Part B.key_evidence: The central position shows a source in the science image and a similarly placed source in the template, while the difference image does not show a clean isolated stellar residual; it is very weak/noisy. The candidate is positive-subtraction, faint (magpsf 21.13), with only 3 detections over 1.23 d. The nearest PS1 source is very close (0.56 arcsec) and non-stellar by sgscore1=0, consistent with a compact galaxy-like counterpart.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: Most consistent with an astrophysical nuclear/host-associated variable, best labeled AGN. The source appears persistent in both science and template rather than a new isolated transient, and there is no obvious science-template positional offset or streak to support a moving object. The very close PS1 counterpart is galaxy-like, favoring variability from a persistent extragalactic source over a Galactic variable star.
Part B.alternative_analysis: A supernova is an alternative if the residual is a faint point source on top of a compact host, but the cutouts do not clearly show a new off-nuclear source or a strong clean positive residual. Bogus is less favored because the science/template source alignment looks coherent rather than a pure subtraction artifact, though the difference image is low S/N.
